data.title: Fox: Mastering Secular Headwinds Via Capital Discipline
data.description:
The Thesis

Fox Corporation (NASDAQ:FOX) (NASDAQ:FOXA) is an old hand at traditional media, but despite the secular headwinds the industry incurs, it still exhibits the characteristics of a resilient cash cow:

  1. moats based on hard-to-replace premium brands like Fox News and live sports rights;
  2. strong cash generation;
  3. return on invested capital consistently above the cost of capital;
  4. disciplined management that does not dilute value with expensive bets on loss-making streaming; and
  5. trading at modest multiples, despite the strong momentum over the past twelve months, with an attractive earnings yield, returning much of the profit via buybacks and dividends.

Thus, I consider FOX, in practice, as a defensive value bet where, although it doesn't grow very fast, it tends to pay well to wait.

Why Fox Continues to Create Shareholder Value

One of the reasons I like Fox as a value pick for the long haul is given it matches the two most important boxes: it generates value for its shareholders (it returns invested capital above the average cost of capital), and it also trades at relatively cheap multiples.

Emphasizing the first point, Fox Corp. makes money today mostly on ads and affiliate fees from cable operators. As an asset-light content/licensing business, it is capital efficient even though it has suffered secular headwinds pushed by cord-cutting and streaming competition. So the reputation of being a business deemed as mature, generating steady cash flows, and very strong in brand, has been put in check and should suffer equally or more than peers in the traditional TV sector.

It turns out that such secular headwinds in the industry are still no match for live TV. Fox News (cable's audience leader) and Fox Sports (NFL and other sports contracts) are the company's "premium" assets, and are harder to replace and more valuable to advertisers. And since Fox sold the movie studios, TV, and content parts of the former 21st Century Fox to Disney (DIS) in 2019, keeping only the essentials, the company without a movie studio, without betting heavily on loss-making streaming, only live content, has managed to generate a lot of cash flow, still well sustained by monetization via cable.

For example, in the last five years, levered free cash flow has been growing at a CAGR of ~8%. In a sense, this matches the idea of a mature cash cow: good cash generation, but not an ultra-growth story.

And given an industry facing secular headwinds, arguably this is a good sign, at least. Behind this cash flow resilience is a Fox that manages to return a robust profit to its shareholders. The capital employed by the company is basically in local broadcasting licenses and sports rights, so Fox doesn't need to reinvest heavily in tangible assets or loss-making streaming.

This resilient cash generation profile translates into a solid ROIC (calculated as NOPAT divided by net working capital + goodwill + other intangibles + long-term investments + deferred tax + other long-term assets), which has been around 10% for the last few years, as you can see in the chart below.

In my view, this is an indication that even without growing aggressively, Fox Corp. manages to monetize every dollar invested relatively well-something essential in an industry facing secular pressures. Although an average ROIC of ~10% (11.3% in the last twelve months) is not spectacular in itself, considering that a risk-free rate of ~4.5 in the U.S., market risk premium, and an extra premium for the traditional media sector, a WACC of ~8% I see as reasonable.

In this case, the ROIC above the WACC for Fox Corp. shows that the company still creates net economic value, with a spread of ~2 p.p. which is not huge, but I would say acceptable for a mature cash cow mainly as long as (1) it doesn't dilute this with bad reinvestments (like unprofitable streaming); and (2) it returns excess cash via dividends and buybacks—which is what Fox does. In the last twelve months alone, $1.2 billion was distributed between buybacks ($1 billion) and dividends ($270 million), that is, a distribution of 5.3% of the current market cap, which I would say is a decent yield, especially for those who see FOX as a bond proxy.

Why FOX’s Valuation Supports the Case

If the first criterion for choosing good companies to own for the long haul is based on excellent fundamentals (moats) that tend to deliver consistent returns above their cost of capital and above their opportunity cost, the second criterion for generating alpha is to determine whether that company is cheaply valued.

To do this, let's first look at FOX valuations through the lens of the operating return the business generates in relation to the total value the market pays for the entire business (equity + net debt), dividing its operating profit over the last twelve months by its enterprise value. Given the $3.02 billion in EBIT generated in that period versus the $27.7 billion in enterprise value, we arrive at an earnings yield (EBIT/EV) of precisely 10.9%.

If EBIT is stable and converted into FCF, this is relatively attractive, especially compared to a WACC of ~8%. The chart below doesn't necessarily show EBIT and FCF generation as super stable, even though I don't see any reason for extreme concern since current values for both EBIT and FCF are above their respective averages shown in the chart. Also, it is expected that in a dynamic industry, volatility should be expected given advertising markets or major sporting events.

At the moment, the earnings yield of ~11% is well above the WACC, and the ROIC of ~10% indicates that the capital employed still generates value. As a large part of the operating profit is converted into free cash flow and returned to the shareholder, the secular risks and recent volatility of EBIT tend to be offset by the capital discipline and the already discounted valuation. Thus, FOX behaves like a defensive cash cow, where the investor is remunerated even without a growth story.

What to Expect From Fox Corp’s FQ4 "Hangover" Earnings?

Fox Corp. is preparing to report its quarterly results on August 5 before the opening bell. To beat estimates, it will need to post EPS numbers above $1.00—which would be annual growth of ~11%—and revenues above $3.12 billion, an annual growth of a little less than ~1%.

While revenue revisions have been maintained by the market over the last six months, EPS revisions have been trimmed by 6.4% over the last three months. Remember that the company will face tough quarterly comps, considering that the massive revenue beat in FQ3 (by $200 million) was due to the Super Bowl bringing in $800 million + in gross ad revenue, in addition to other highlights such as Tubi growing organically by 35% YoY and a strong Cable Network ad revenue growing 26% YoY.

However, despite the increase in revenue (helped by one-off events such as the Super Bowl), the sharp drop in operating margin and EBITDA in the TV division may have signaled higher costs. Television EBITDA collapsed from $145 million to $60 million, as the cost of rights and production significantly expanded, leading to consolidated EBITDA margins of 19.6% versus 25.6% in the same period last year.

But my view is that without the high-cost Super Bowl rights dragging down TV EBITDA, margins in the TV segment should rebound somewhat in FQ4. I also see Cable Networks should stay stable with solid political ad tailwind as well as Tubi still dilutes margin a bit (low margin vs linear TV), but it's still a net positive for top-line.

So I see the downward revisions in FOX's EPS as a reflection of a combination of compressed margins, but potentially FQ4 bringing strong cash flows, as pointed out by the management team, given seasonal working capital issues, with advertising receivables to be reversed from the second half of this year.

But all in all, I'd say the odds are more in FOX's favor to deliver an "ok" result in FQ4, followed by a seasonal "hangover", with weaker quarterly revenues (without the Super Bowl), but potentially more stable or slightly improving margins and profits, supported by Tubi's growth and Cable's stability.

Risks and Final Remarks

It's not that there aren't structural risks for Fox Corp. (audience migration to streamign, cost of sports rights only growing, and Tubi still needing to prove significant profits), but I see this scenario as already known, and not a new factor that overturns the thesis in FQ4.

But naturally, as greater volatility in terms of operating profit pressured by such secular industry headwinds may come to punish FOX Corp. eventually, its defensive moat that protects consistent returns on invested capital tends to be the main point to monitor.

That said, I would summarize the Fox Corp. thesis as a defensive bet on a traditional media business that, despite secular challenges, still manages to generate economic value above its cost of capital, convert much of its operating profit into free cash flow, and return that cash to shareholders in a disciplined manner.

With an ROIC consistently above WACC, an attractive earnings yield relative to risk, and premium brands that are difficult to replicate—Fox News and the live sports rights—FOX behaves like a resilient cash cow in a mature sector.

data.title: Mastercard Q2 Preview: All Eyes On Stablecoins
data.description:
Between traditional and digital

Mastercard ($MA) is no longer a credit card network, but a strategic global infrastructure, increasingly close to becoming a hybrid platform between traditional and digital finance. There is still some appreciation for the stock, which the market considers “boring,” premium but predictable, good for riding the wave of consumer growth and little else. My thesis is different: I believe that Mastercard is building, beneath the surface, a set of still undervalued options that could radically change the stock's trajectory in the coming years. From the penetration of stablecoins (already regulated in Europe with USDG) to AI-based integration in the fight against fraud, to expansion into real-time payments, Mastercard is planting the right seeds at a time when the narrative on Big Tech is beginning to revolve around infrastructure and no longer just content.

It is certainly not a stock that will double in value. But it is one of the few cases where fundamental quality (margins >58%, FCF conversion above 50%, clear pricing power) is accompanied by evolving strategic leverage. And for a medium-term investor, it is precisely this mix (visibility + optionality) that represents an interesting combination.

One network, two souls

Those who view Mastercard from afar tend to simplify it: it is a card circuit, period. But the reality is much more interesting than one might think. Today, 40% of revenues come from value-added solutions that go far beyond swiping a card. We are talking about cybersecurity, anti-fraud AI, digital identity solutions, and open banking. And this second soul (less visible, but increasingly relevant) is what I believe will drive the revaluation of the stock in the coming years.

The core business remains very solid: the Payment Network segment, based on GDV volumes and switching fees, generates over 60% of revenues and maintains textbook operating margins (58.5% compared to an industry average of 34%). The acceptance network is vast: 150 million physical locations and 250 million digital touchpoints. But it is the technological infrastructure that is evolving rapidly. Following the acquisition of Recorded Future for $2.7 billion, Mastercard has enhanced its real-time threat intelligence capabilities, integrating them into risk management and AI-based services offered to partner banks.

Geographically, diversification is also robust: approximately 45% of revenues come from North America, with the rest balanced between EMEA and Asia-Pacific, with above-average growth in India and Southeast Asia. It is one of the few names in the sector that can grow both in developed markets (thanks to the upsell of digital services) and in emerging markets (thanks to financial inclusion and physical expansion).

Q2 2025 Earnings Preview: Strength Lies in the Details

For the second quarter of 2025, the Bloomberg consensus expects adjusted EPS of $4.02 and revenues of $7.94 billion, with an operating margin of around 59.3%. These are solid numbers, confirming the double-digit growth trajectory (YoY revenue +9.5%, cross-border +15.5%), but they do not tell the whole story.

Mastercard is not in the spotlight today because the market almost takes it for granted that it will “do its job.” The real issue is not beating the consensus but reigniting the narrative about what Mastercard is becoming, namely a high-margin infrastructure operator that combines payments, AI, digital identity, and stablecoins.

Personally, I won't be impressed by a $0.05 beat on EPS. I will focus on:

  • the dynamics of revenue from value-added services, especially those related to security, real-time payments, and identity (Recorded Future);
  • any comments on the expansion of the USDG project in the US, following its European launch with Robinhood and Kraken;
  • the evolution of client incentives, which are expected to reach $4.9 billion: an increase in line with the strategy of penetration among large merchants.

Furthermore, the quality of the beat (if it comes) will matter more than the beat itself. If volumes rise but take-rates come under pressure, or if margins are squeezed due to expansion costs in stablecoins and AI, the market could react coldly. But if the company proves it can sustain growth while maintaining control of operating leverage, Mastercard could be revalued more as an “AI-enabled fintech infrastructure stock” than as a defensive stock in payments.

That's why Mastercard could be worth $650

When I look at Mastercard, I don't just see a payments company: I see a margin machine, with a business model that prints cash in every market cycle. Unlike many of the more talked-about growth stocks, we're talking about a company with expected ROE above 200% as early as 2025 and an EBITDA margin exceeding 60%. At these levels of efficiency, the multiple is not a risk: it's a consequence.

Based on Bloomberg estimates, I have built a valuation model starting from an expected EBITDA of $19.646 billion for 2025.

Considering Mastercard's characteristics (duopolistic leadership, expansion of cross-border volumes, and pricing power on value-added services) I applied an EV/EBITDA multiple of 32x, consistent with the high end of historical valuations.

So:

  • Enterprise value (EV): $19.646 billion × 32 = $628.672 billion
  • Expected net debt: approximately $9 billion, therefore equity value = $619.672 billion
  • Diluted shares outstanding: approximately 951 million
  • Fair value per share = approximately $650

This assessment does not claim to be perfect, but it reflects a very simple idea. In my opinion, Mastercard deserves a premium because it is turning every evolution in payments into a competitive advantage. And if cash flows remain this strong, the market will return to paying it what it is worth.

Personal note: Personally, I still see the 32x multiple as justified. The new wave of AI-driven solutions, integration with regulated stablecoins, and increased revenues from value-added services are not yet fully reflected in the numbers. And Mastercard (as it often does) could surprise on the upside in the next two quarters.

Stablecoins, antitrust, and new challenges

It's true, Mastercard seems like a fortress today. But even fortresses need to update their defenses. The stablecoin market – considered a marginal issue for years – has suddenly become a strategic front, and Mastercard is neck-deep in it. The news that Amazon and Walmart are evaluating proprietary payment solutions based on stablecoins radically changes the competitive landscape. It is no longer a war between networks, but between models.

Mastercard has responded by forging alliances with players such as Robinhood, Fiserv, and Paxos to facilitate the adoption of regulated stablecoins (USDG, FIUSD) in compliance with the new European MiCA regulatory framework. But the truth is that if these initiatives take hold, Mastercard risks seeing part of its volume slip outside its perimeter, bypassing the traditional circuit altogether. At least, that is the fear hanging over the market.

And then there is the antitrust issue, which recurs cyclically. The recent legal victory in the US against a lawsuit alleging collusion with Apple was a positive sign, but the litigation in the UK (with over 500 retailers involved in the interchange fees case) remains an open wound. It is difficult to say whether all this will lead to sanctions, fee revisions, or simply background noise, but we cannot ignore that part of the long-term narrative, that of the efficiency of the duopoly, is now under scrutiny.

Personally, I believe that anyone investing in Mastercard today must accept the idea that its competitive advantage is less secure than it seems. But that does not make it any less profitable. If the company manages to transform the stablecoin risk into a new interoperable infrastructure, then we are not talking about a threat, but about evolution. And Mastercard has already proven, historically, that it knows how to evolve better than anyone else.

The real strength of Mastercard

Many focus solely on GDV (gross dollar volume) or transaction fees. But the reality is that Mastercard has built a second growth engine in recent years, one that is much less visible but much more stable: value-added services. Today, these represent almost 40% of total revenue and continue to expand faster than the traditional business.

We are talking about cybersecurity, tokenization, identity verification, open banking, real-time payment solutions, and even analytics for banks and merchants. These are all high-margin, non-cyclical areas that strengthen customer relationships. If the world stopped using physical cards tomorrow, Mastercard would still have a pipeline of organically growing services.

In my opinion, this is where the stock's rerating potential lies: no longer just as a “network,” but as a full-stack payment infrastructure. The recent acquisition of Recorded Future for $2.7 billion is a step in this direction: it integrates advanced threat intelligence into payment flows, bringing Mastercard to the heart of real-time decision automation.

Risks and opportunities

No brand is immune. Not even Mastercard. And anyone who ignores the risks that this business model entails has failed to understand where we are headed. The first and most obvious risk is regulatory. From British courts to the European Commission to the US Congress, large payment networks are increasingly under scrutiny. The recent fine imposed by the Bank of England on Vocalink is the latest example. Not so much for the amount ($16 million), but for the signal it sends: regulators are now also monitoring infrastructure operations, not just fees.

At the macro level, the second risk is the compression of transaction volumes in a scenario of global economic slowdown. Mastercard is more exposed than Visa to cross-border transactions, and this worked very well during the post-COVID reopening. But if global growth slows down (and the IMF has already revised its estimates downward), it will be difficult to maintain the same pace. Added to this is a technological risk that is often mentioned but rarely fully understood: disintermediation via stablecoins.

Amazon and Walmart are exploring the creation of their own digital currencies. The same goes for Robinhood with USDG, backed by Mastercard, Kraken, and Paxos. And the US Congress has already passed a framework law (the GENIUS Act). In other words, the future is no longer “if,” but “when.”

Personally, I don't believe that stablecoins will destroy the Mastercard model, quite the contrary. BUT companies that fail to integrate them into their stack risk being marginalized. Mastercard is trying to move ahead of the curve: it has entered into partnerships with Fiserv, Chainlink, Paxos, and ZeroHash to test interoperability between fiat and stablecoins. This is a smart attempt to integrate the threat before it becomes systemic.

Not everything is at risk. There are also underestimated opportunities. The first? Strengthening its position in emerging markets, where digital payment penetration is still low but growth is explosive. In India, in particular, Mastercard is forging strategic partnerships, aware that the future of cashless payments lies with the next 3 billion digital users.

The second opportunity concerns high-margin services, often ignored by retail investors. As mentioned above, these currently account for 40% of revenues, but could exceed 50% within 2-3 years. And finally, the real “gem”: Mastercard is quietly becoming a data infrastructure, with expansion into the segments of fraud detection, alternative scoring, and cybersecurity that could transform it into a strategic asset for governments and central banks as well. A bit like Palantir, but without the hype risk.

Why Mastercard deserves trust

The market has now realized that Mastercard is no longer a transaction processor. It is an infrastructure. It is a critical node in the global payment system. And, perhaps even more importantly, it is one of the few operators capable of adapting before the revolution arrives. It does not suffer from the issue of stablecoins: it studies it, experiments with it, and incorporates it. It does not ignore regulatory pressure: it faces it head-on, even at the cost of paying fines. Margins, even in this challenging environment, remain very high. And cash generation is so solid that it allows for constant buybacks and growing dividends without impacting financial leverage.

With estimated EBITDA for 2025 of $19.6 billion and an extremely solid net financial position, we have estimated a fair value of around $615 per share, implying a potential upside of +15% from current levels. In a more favorable scenario, with acceleration in value-added services, re-acceleration of cross-border transactions, and stable growth in e-commerce in emerging countries, the stock could even reach $650, a threshold already mentioned above as the implicit “upper bound” in several valuation models.

But the real gem? Mastercard is quietly building a global graph of digital identities and payment habits. Its assets in behavioral data, authentication solutions, and fraud prevention could become a new standard for governments and central banks grappling with digital identity over the next five years. This is not science fiction: it has already signed strategic partnerships with countries such as Singapore, the United Kingdom, and Canada on these fronts.

This is the part of the story that the market is perhaps still underestimating. And that we can take advantage of today.

  • Rating: Buy
  • Target: $615
  • Ideal weight in portfolio in a balanced: 3–5%, accumulate on pullbacks below $520 (there, strong buy)

data.title: Mapfre, S.A. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Mapfre, S.A. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Popular: An Attractively Priced Bet On Puerto Rico's Economy
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, Popular (NASDAQ:BPOP) has outperformed the iShares U.S. Financials ETF (IYF), delivering a gain of ~25%:

I think the outperformance is likely to continue, with Popular trading at only 9.4x consensus 2026 earnings, a circa 25% discount to the regional banking peer group.

In this article, I discuss Popular's Q2 2025 operating performance, its 2025 outlook, as well as how Fed rate cuts may impact the bank's vast investment portfolio. I ultimately rate Popular a Buy, notwithstanding risks stemming primarily from its outsized exposure to Puerto Rico.

Company Overview

You can access all BPOP results on its website here. Popular is a U.S. regional bank focused primarily on Puerto Rico (80% of all assets), with the remainder of the balance sheet deployed primarily in the Contiguous United States, but also the Caribbean:

Popular exhibits a low loan-to-deposit ratio of just 57%, with the excess deposits deployed primarily in investment securities. The loan portfolio is split between Commercial loans (50% of the loan portfolio), followed by mortgages at 22%:

Net interest income accounted for 79% of Q2 2025 revenues, with Popular's asset management and insurance businesses contributing to a marginally higher (compared with other regional banks) 21% revenue exposure to non-interest income.

Q2 2025 Results Overview

Popular delivered EPS of $3.09/share in Q2 2025, up 26% Y/Y, benefitting from a higher net interest margin (+0.27% to 3.22%) and a lower share count (-6.1% Y/Y):

Q2 2025 credit loss provisions were only marginally higher Y/Y at $49 million, and significantly lower than the $64 million booked in Q1 2025, resulting in a significant 21% Q/Q increase in EPS, taking H1 2025 earnings to $5.64/share, up 45% Y/Y.

The bank remains well capitalized with a CET1 ratio of 15.91%, although it is marginally lower both Q/Q and Y/Y as a result of ongoing share repurchases.

Updated 2025 Outlook

Popular also boosted its full-year 2025 guidance with regards to net interest income growth, now seen at 10-11% Y/Y (previously 7-9%), with non-interest income also expected at the higher end of the $155-160 million guidance. Loan growth is still expected at 3-5% Y/Y, while EPS will see a marginal benefit from a lower 18-20% effective tax rate (previously 19-21%):

Overall, I think that Popular has navigated the recent U.S. economic uncertainty well, with the cost of risk expected to remain low in the remainder of 2025.

Coupled with ongoing share repurchases (new $500 million repurchase program announced in July 2025), I think that Popular will beat consensus EPS estimates of $10.46/share in 2025:

The investment portfolio

In light of the low 57% loan-to-deposit ratio, paying attention to Popular's outsized investment portfolio can provide useful information with regards to the future evolution of the bank's net interest income.

We observe that the biggest mark-to-market loss stems from Agency securities investments made at a yield of only 1.8%. These account for 14.8% of the total portfolio.

The other loss of $387 million, which is not recognized in Popular's balance sheet because of its held-to-maturity treatment, stems from U.S. treasuries investments at a very low 1.3% yield, with remaining maturity of two years:

In summary, we can say that 36.5% of the investment portfolio is invested in securities which will provide higher net interest income once they mature and are reinvested at presently higher interest rates.

Future Fed policy

The other key point to make about the securities portfolio is that 41.4% of the portfolio is invested in overnight/very short duration instruments such as treasury bills. These investments offer yields closely correlated with the Fed funds rate.

Looking at Fed funds futures prices, we observe that the Fed funds rate is seen at about 3.25% in December 2026, in essence implying four, potentially five 0.25% Fed rate cuts in the next 17 months:

As a result, given the outsized 41.4% exposure of Popular's investment portfolio to the Fed funds rate, I expect net interest income from the securities portfolio to marginally decline in 2026 and going into 2027, only partially offset by the reinvestment of maturing loss-making securities (36.5% of the portfolio as explained above).

Limited impact on loan yields

On a more positive note, it is encouraging to see that Popular's loan yield is only down 0.02% to 7.50% at the end of Q2 2025, indicating little impact from the 1% in Fed rate cuts late in 2024:

As such, the net ultimate effect of upcoming Fed interest rate cuts on Popular's consolidated net interest income will depend largely on loan growth dynamics and whether the bank takes more duration risk by moving some part of the 41.4% investment portfolio directly exposed to Fed rate cuts into higher-yielding but also higher duration U.S. treasuries.

Popular valuation

Popular stock currently demands a 1.53x premium to its Q2 2025 tangible book value of $75.41/share, reflecting its strong deposit franchise and robust current profitability.

The bank trades at just 9.4x its 2026 consensus earnings of $12.25/share (which imply earnings growth of 17% relative to the 2025 earnings consensus of $10.46/share). The latest buyback alone should be enough to reduce share count by 6%, indicating that underlying profitability needs to contribute the remaining ~10% EPS improvement.

I think this profitability improvement will largely depend on whether Popular is aggressive in redeploying part of its overnight Fed funds exposure to longer-duration securities, which admittedly expose the bank to losses should the Fed have to tighten policy above its 3% neutral rate in the future.

Banks held in the SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE) currently trade at 11.9x their forward earnings, implying that Popular's 9.4x forward multiple represents a circa 25% discount relative to its direct peer group.

As such, even if Popular takes a conservative approach to its investment portfolio and does not add duration risk (i.e., likely resulting in lower net interest income), I think that the current discount relative to regional bank peers provides a wide margin of safety, earning Popular a Buy rating on my part.

Risks

In light of Popular's 80% exposure to Puerto Rico, developments in the territory's economy can make or break the bank's fortunes. A recent discussion organized by the Financial Oversight & Management Board for Puerto Rico flagged several risks to the territory's economy:

  • Weak economic growth between 0.4-1.2% in 2025, potentially slowing to just 0.1% in 2026.
  • High dependence on Federal Funding, which has recently become more uncertain.
  • Elevated inflation, coupled with lackluster growth, results in limited growth in consumers' purchasing power, as evidenced by rising delinquencies in credit card and auto loan payments.

Looking at Popular's auto loan and credit card portfolios, we see little evidence of rising delinquencies. In fact, in Q2 2025, delinquencies are down Y/Y for both loan classes, standing at 4.29% for auto loans and 4.01% for credit cards, respectively:

As such, even if current credit quality for Popular remains robust, future deterioration driven by weakness in Puerto Rico's economy cannot be ruled out.

The second key risk to consider would be a recession in the United States, which would prompt the Fed to cut interest rates even faster, potentially below its 3% neutral rate. Such a scenario could be devastating for Popular as 41.4% of the investment portfolio is invested at the overnight Fed funds rate.

The good news is that investment banks have recently dialed back their expectations for a U.S. recession. Case in point, Goldman Sachs (GS) now sees just 30% odds of a recession over the next 12 months, down from 45% in April 2025.

Conclusion

Popular delivered strong EPS growth both Y/Y and Q/Q, benefiting from an improvement in its key net interest margin, ongoing share repurchases, and a benign credit environment.

Coupled with the updated guidance, this puts the bank in a good position to beat 2025 analyst EPS consensus, with results in 2026 more dependent on how Popular reshuffles its vast investment portfolio amid expected Fed rate cuts.

The bank currently trades at only 9.4x its 2026 consensus earnings, a circa 25% discount to its regional bank peer group. I think this provides a good margin of safety, earning Popular a Buy rating from me.

Investors should consider risks stemming from Popular's outsized exposure to Puerto Rico, as well as the possibility of a U.S. recession prompting the Fed to cut interest rates below neutral.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Schneider Electric: Firing On All Cylinders, But Shares Are Not Cheap
data.description:
Schneider Electric (OTCPK:SBGSF)(OTCPK:SBGSY) has gone from being a relatively mediocre industrial company to a wonderful business over the past decade. Its returns on invested capital (ROIC) and returns on capital employed (ROCE) have trended higher, its recurring revenue has increased, and so has the contribution from software subscriptions.

It has become an essential partner to many construction and infrastructure companies, such as data centers and renewable plant developers. As the company has gone from selling components to selling solutions, its pricing power has increased, and there is a higher switching cost for customers, widening Schneider Electric's competitive moat. We wrote an article in 2022 opining that it was a great time to buy shares, and since then they have more than doubled. Early this year we wrote another article where we changed our rating to "Hold," and since then the total returns have been similar to the S&P 500 Index (SP500). The company appears to be firing on all cylinders, so we thought now is a good time to take another look at the company.

First Quarter Earnings Results

Despite a tough environment for industrial companies (XLI), Schneider Electric delivered solid first quarter 2025 results. The company should announce second quarter results at the end of July, and based on the results delivered by competitors like ABB (OTCPK:ABBNY) it is likely that the company will deliver another strong quarter. Capital expenditures remain extremely weak in Europe and North America, but companies like Schneider and ABB have benefited from their exposure to high-growth infrastructure segments like data centers and renewable energy.

Schneider Electric delivered sales of €9.3 billion in the first quarter, representing organic growth of close to 7.4% compared to the previous year. Still, the Industrial Automation segment was not immune to CapEx weakness and delivered organic growth of -1%. This was more than compensated by the much larger Energy Management segment, which accomplished organic growth of more than 10%.

Financials

There was a clear inflection point in Schneider Electric's revenue growth after the pandemic, although it has moderated after hitting annual year-over-year growth of 19%+. Another metric trending higher is the company's operating margin, which is now over 200 basis points above its ten-year average. Given the company's strategy we described at the start of the article and operating leverage, we see room for further margin expansion. With both revenue and margins increasing, it is not a surprise that earnings per share (EPS) have grown at a very healthy rate.

M&A

Another lever that Schneider Electric has pulled to add extra growth is executing targeted bolt-on acquisitions. This includes recent news that the company is considering purchasing the remaining stake owned by others in its Indian subsidiary. This is important, as India has emerged as Schneider's third-largest market.

Recent acquisitions show the company's strategy to increase its share of wallet in the end-markets it is already seeing the most success, such as data centers. For example, Motivair is a liquid cooling and thermal management solution provider for data centers, and will be consolidated in the Energy Management business. Schneider Electric is also resorting to joint ventures (JVs), such as the recently announced JV with eStart to combine both companies' expertise in the energy storage and EV Charging infrastructure.

Balance Sheet

Schneider Electric has a robust balance sheet with billions in cash and short-term investments that covers almost half of its total long-term debt. While the company has a net debt position, its leverage is moderate, and the company has the capacity to continue making bolt-on acquisitions as opportunities emerge.

Peer Comparison

Schneider Electric maintains a very strong competitive position in the global market for energy management and industrial automation solutions, thanks to its comprehensive and digitally integrated solutions. It is particularly strong in solutions tailored specifically to sustainability and energy efficiency.

Schneider has also differentiated itself by offering broader digital capabilities and more robust solutions for smart grid infrastructure and renewable energy integration compared to peers like Eaton Corporation (ETN), Emerson Electric (EMR), and the ABB Group. This has positioned the company well to grow faster, which is reflected in the highest five-year revenue compounded annual growth rate (CAGR) in the peer group.

Still, all these companies dominate in certain niches, and they all have attractive profit margins. This has resulted in elevated valuations, with P/E GAAP (TTM) ratios of ~30x or more. Other valuation metrics such as EV/EBITDA (TTM) and Price/Cash Flow (TTM) appear stretched as well for all these companies.

Future Outlook

Schneider reiterated during the most recent earnings call its full year 2025 guidance, which calls for adjusted EBITDA of 10% to 15%, driven by revenue growth of between 7% and 10% and EBITDA margin improvement of 50 to 80 basis points. Management expressed strong confidence in the outlook given their strong pipeline and backlog.

The company expects particularly strong demand to continue from the data center and networks end-market, with continuing weakness in the residential end-market. It also expects demand recovery in discrete automation, particularly in the second half of 2025. Schneider also expects further progress on the subscription transition in its software offerings, and strong growth in Services. The company is taking commercial and supply chain actions to mitigate tariff impacts.

Looking further ahead, Schneider Electric aims to become the undisputed leader in Electrification and Digitalization technologies, by providing innovative solutions for renewable energy generation, grid modernization, and energy storage. It also plans to increase software integration by leveraging its AVEVA acquisition, which should help provide customers with software that enables greater control, efficiency, and resilience. Schneider is well positioned to benefit from secular tailwinds such as AI data centers, and the automation and electrification of houses, buildings, and factories.

Valuation

At first glance, Schneider Electric appears expensive, trading at roughly 50% above its historical valuation metrics. Still, Schneider is evolving toward a high-margin, technology-driven company with higher recurrent revenue streams. In that sense, a premium valuation to its historical multiples is warranted, but still, the current valuation already reflects high-single-digit revenue growth expectations and some margin expansion. This means there is little valuation margin of safety, and therefore we see the company as a "Hold."

Risks

The list of risks for Schneider Electric is long and includes tariffs, geopolitics, inflation, foreign exchange risks, supply chain risks, and strong competition. These are mitigated by the company's strong competitive moat and customer relations, a solid balance sheet, and product and geographic diversification.

Still, we would note that North America represents more than a third of the company's business, which means tariffs and the U.S. economy have a strong weight on the company's financial performance. Other risks include reduced incentives for renewable energy and electric vehicles (EVs) from governments, and relatively weak residential markets in Western Europe and North America.

Conclusion

Schneider Electric continues to widen the moat, be it through strategic bolt-on acquisitions or by expanding and integrating more software within its products to offer comprehensive solutions and generate recurrent revenue. By integrating itself more with end users' operations and strengthening customer relationships, switching costs increase for the customer, making it less likely that they will leave the company for a competitor.

This strategy of moving away from transactional sales toward stickier recurring revenue has reduced cyclicality, increased profit margins, and widened the competitive moat. With consistent revenue and earnings growth, there is much to like, except perhaps the valuation, which continues to be relatively stretched. As much as we appreciate Schneider Electric, and believe it is a well-run and high-quality business, at current prices we see shares as a "Hold". Especially considering the very complex environment with above-average risks, ranging from tariffs and inflation to mixed signals from the economy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Materialise NV 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Materialise NV in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: T-Mobile Q2 Earnings: Convergence Changes The Game
data.description:
I am updating my previous analysis on T-Mobile (NASDAQ:TMUS) in light of Q2 2025 earnings which were released post-market on Wednesday, July 23rd. I previously rated T-Mobile a sell for the following reasons:

  • DCF-analysis supported a price target of $226, 14% downside to pricing at the time.
  • The industry was on the verge of price wars as consumer confidence fell and household debt increased
  • Industry forecasts were already being scaled back and T-Mobile's ARPU was leveling off

Since then, T-Mobile has returned 9% while the S&P 500 has returned over 12%.

I have been bearish on T-Mobile the last few quarters, not because of the company's strength, but because of the elevated share price. After two quarters in a row of exceptional performance, it was already getting hard to maintain this stance. But specifically digging into convergence results on top of higher cash flow guidance, my DCF analysis also supports a more positive rating.

At a price target of $287, 15% upside from today's pricing and revised up from my prior price target of $226, I raise my rating from sell to buy.

Q2 Earnings Recap

T-Mobile had another exceptional quarter with EPS of $2.86 beating consensus by $0.17.

The business really outperformed from a volume standpoint with postpaid metrics and 5G broadband metrics up both sequentially and year-over-year. The 5G performance especially caught my attention and drives the focus of the rest of my analysis.

The biggest news was T-Mobile revising guidance upward, signaling that Q2 was not an outlier but a continuation of a trend.

Given the economic and market risks facing T-Mobile, I was truly impressed by the earnings across the board and it has caused me to revisit my assumptions and scrub everything from top to bottom.

Valuation

I updated my ongoing DCF analysis for T-Mobile with the following assumptions:

  • 5% near-term revenue growth based on guidance, trends, and convergence trajectory
  • 3% near-term cost growth based on inflation forecast and mid-point of management guidance
  • 2.7% long-run growth rate, one percentage point below the market CAGR to hedge for scale of cost base and declining returns over time
  • 7% discount rate based on 6.8% estimated WACC
  • Consistent with management guidance, I included Metronet and excluded US Cellular

This DCF analysis yields a price target of $287, 15% upside from today's pricing and up from my prior price target of $226.

Wall Street is also optimistic with a price target of $269, more than 7% upside to today's pricing. I noted that my price target is still within the overall Wall Street range.

The quant rating is at strong buy driven by T-Mobile's exception growth and profitability the last few quarters.

Convergence Has Changed The Game

My bear case for T-Mobile had been the risk of a price war outweighing the upside from the company's strong execution. With the US Telecom market forecast to grow just a little ahead of inflation, price war risk is high in what is essentially a 3-player postpaid market.

While that risk still exists, it is impossible to ignore T-Mobile's execution. And what really changes my thesis is T-Mobile's performance in convergence with their 5G broadband product. I use this AT&T (T) chart far too often but I love the clear story. Convergence not only drives growth, it drives happier and stickier customers which is critical in a slow growth environment.

T-Mobile's broadband business is not only accelerating from a net addition standpoint, they are experiencing lower churn in alignment with AT&Ts research above. This is especially significant as, unlike AT&T and Verizon (VZ), T-Mobile doesn't have an existing broadband offer so this represents incremental accounts on lower invested capital.

I had also been worried about promotions and price wars as ARPA and ARPU showed weakness in Q4 2024 and Q1 2025. While we are not out of the woods yet, T-Mobile was able to return to growth. This means they were able to overcome promotional activity with premium services, while also not giving up the farm to grow broadband.

With no legacy copper decay to worry about, and essentially pure upside from 5G broadband, the trends in T-Mobile have changed the game raising both my revenue growth expectations as well as cash flow expectations. All of this aligns with managements guidance for 2025, but I am comfortable extrapolating this to outer years based on my convergence research and the overall market expectations.

Downside Risk

Downside risk continues to stem from price wars which I am closely monitoring. Historical price wars have impacted telecom companies, besides the obvious "lower revenue." The last major pricing action for wireless was in the '16 to '17 timeframe.

While T-Mobile was smaller and pre-Sprint at the time, AT&T and Verizon both saw a material drop in normalized net income, that was largely not recovered until the '21 to '22 timeframe. So historically, major price wars have had bottom-line impacts with multi-year recovery.

So why the risk of a price war now? Consumer confidence in the US fell significantly in February 2025 as a result of uncertainty around mass government layoffs alongside tariff policy. Household debt has also increased, signaling consumers are being strapped financially.

Industry observers also expect the telecom industry to have a bumpy ride. In a recent study from Deloitte, Fixed Wireless Access (a major growth business for the major telecoms) is expected to slow.

While FWA is expected to expand globally, providing connectivity to millions, the US market will likely experience more measured growth.

I believe T-Mobile is best positioned to weather the storm, but I still intend to keep a close eye on ARPU and promotion activity.

Verdict

It is impossible to ignore T-Mobile's outsized performance relative to competitors. This comes in addition to exceptional performance in convergence as the 5G broadband business accelerates. While price wars continue to be a concern, increased guidance and improved trends swing the math bullish on T-Mobile.

At a price target of $287, 15% upside from today's pricing and revised up from my prior price target of $226, I raise my rating from sell to buy.

data.title: CNX Resources Corporation 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by CNX Resources Corporation in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Fidelity Growth Company Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares (MUTF:FDGRX) gained 18.97%, outpacing the 17.55% advance of the benchmark, the Russell 3000* Growth Index. As always, we focused on firms operating in well-positioned industries and niches that we believe are capable of delivering persistent sales and earnings growth. Long-term performance continues to favor the fund over the benchmark. U.S. growth stocks ended June at an all-time high after erasing a brief but steep early-April decline that followed President Trump's announcement of a sweeping tariff plan, resulting in rising volatility and concern about a global trade war. The benchmark returned -12.75% from April 2 through April 8, but then sharply reversed course and rose 32.41% through midyear.

The historically fast rebound was set off by a 90-day pause on most planned tariffs, and sustained by robust corporate profits, a solid economy and inflation trending around the Federal Reserve's 2% target. These tailwinds overshadowed rising tension in the Middle East and lingering uncertainty about trade ahead of the July 8 expiration of the tariff pause.

The Fed held steady its benchmark federal funds rate in May and June, as expected, stating it is open to cutting rates in the second half of the year but prefers a wait-and-see stance for the time being, given its expectation for meaningful inflation in the coming months, as well as uncertainty about the impact of tariffs on prices. The central bank cut its projection for U.S. economic growth this year and raised its outlook for inflation and unemployment.

For the quarter, growth stocks handily outpaced value, as the Russell 3000* Value Index gained 3.84%. Accordingly, the index's gain was concentrated in the growth-oriented information technology ( +25% ) and communication services ( +24% ) sectors, mostly reflecting the potential for generative artificial intelligence to drive transformative change. These two groups combined represented about 60% of the index for the three months. Stocks in the consumer discretionary and industrials sectors, which typically move in step with the economy, each rose 14 %.

In contrast, energy ( -7% ) - a very small index component - lagged by the widest margin, as the price of oil rose. The defensive health care sector also lost ground, returning roughly -1 %.

Stock selection meaningfully contributed to the fund's performance versus the benchmark for the quarter, led by our picks in the information technology and financials sectors.

In tech, a large overweight in Nvidia ( +46% ) was by far the top individual contributor. On May 28, the dominant maker of advanced graphics chips for generative AI systems reported sharp increases in sales and profit for the three months ending April 27. Revenue surged 69% to a new quarterly record, even though the company was effectively shut out of China, one of the largest markets for its products. Nvidia's data-center business grew even faster, rising 73% due to strong demand for chips and other components used in AI computing systems. Given our positive view of Nvidia's growth prospects, the stock ended June as the top holding and overweight by a wide margin.

Also in tech, it helped to hold a non-benchmark stake in CoreWeave, which specializes in providing cloud-based graphics processing unit infrastructure to AI developers. The position gained 335% the past three months, which roughly matched the period since its March 28 initial public offering. In mid-May, the company reported better-than-expected quarterly revenue, and management guided second-quarter sales higher than Wall Street's consensus expectation, reflecting strong growth driven by increased adoption of AI by enterprises.

In contrast, our decision to underweight chipmaker Broadcom (AVGO) ( +65% ) was the largest individual relative detractor. We preferred to overweight Nvidia and other semiconductor stocks. On June 5, the company announced that revenue and sales for the three months ending May 4 were slightly ahead of consensus expectations, driven by its custom chips and software that speed up AI computations. Also, management's revenue forecast for its next quarter exceeded analysts' forecasts. We'll note that we modestly added to Broadcom this quarter, making it a fairly sizable fund holding, but it was the second-largest underweight as of midyear.

An overweight in athletic apparel maker lululemon athletica (-16%) hurt relative performance. The stock fell sharply in June after the company warned its profit would be held back by macro challenges. We reduced the fund's stake in Q2, which nonetheless remained an overweight as of June 30.

* 1 basis point =0.01 %.

Outlook and Positioning

As the second half of 2025 begins, the global economy appears to be on steadier ground than many investors had anticipated and the near-term outlook for expansion looks favorable, but policy uncertainty is high, especially related to tariffs. Much of the actual and potential impact of tariff policy is ahead, in our view, and we recognize that in the past high policy uncertainty has tended to coincide with elevated market volatility.

The Fed, in a wait-and-see stance after reducing rates by 1 percentage point in 2024, is leaning toward more cuts, and a sweeping, pro-cyclical domestic policy bill is nearing passage.

Nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury yields finished June about where they began the quarter, around 4.2 %. Inflation remained higher than the Fed's 2% target, as sticky prices in services and housing offset deflation in goods.

Inflation has come down significantly from its 2022 peak, but we believe more-robust measures of the Fed's preferred inflation metric indicate persistent inflationary pressure under the surface. We expect inflation to remain rangebound around 3% into next year, with upside risk from tariff hikes.

The U.S. dollar dropped to a three-year low in Q2, extending its year-to-date decline to roughly 10%. Labor trends, including strong wage growth and a surge in consumption spending, moderated the past three months, as cuts to federal government funding, layoffs and rising uncertainty led to slower spending and a decline in consumer sentiment.

Business investment remained positive, spurred by outlays for Al by large companies in the information technology and communication services sectors. After weakening in Q1, the consensus estimate for 2025 corporate earnings growth stabilized, while profit expectations were robust.

Against this backdrop, we are focusing on investments with a favorable outlook, even amid modest economic growth, while noting additional risks are probable. We plan to be more selective than ever in choosing stocks.

In all sectors, the fund holds companies with above-benchmark, long-term growth in sales and earnings per share, reflecting our view that growth for advantaged companies can persist for years. As a result, changes to the fund's positioning were modest in Q2, although the June rebalance of the fund's benchmark impacted some relative sector weightings.

For instance, although we slightly added to the fund's allocation to information technology, the sector ended June as a modest underweight. Still, the sector remained by far the largest area of investment, at 49% of assets.

Within tech, the fund holds above-benchmark holdings in niche companies that appear to be attractive, secular growers. This is partially offset by underweights in some larger, established companies that appear fairly valued and without compelling longterm growth prospects.

The second-largest area of investment was consumer discretionary, at 15% of assets, including key investments in athletic footwear and apparel makers that are executing well, as well as online goods and service providers taking share from physical merchants.

As of midyear, the fund's largest overweights were in health care, consumer discretionary and communication services, whereas the biggest underweights were industrials and financials.

Investment Approach

  • Fidelity" Growth Company Fund is a domestic equity strategy that invests across a spectrum of companies, from blue chip to aggressive growth.
  • Our investment approach is anchored by the philosophy that the market often underestimates the duration of a company's growth, particularly in cases where the resiliency and extensibility of the business model are underappreciated.
  • We focus on firms operating in well-positioned industries and niches that we believe are capable of delivering persistent sales and earnings growth.
  • This approach typically leads us to companies that we think have the potential to unlock shareholder value through either a growth-enhancing product cycle or an internal catalyst, such as a turnaround or an acquisition.
  • We believe it critical that companies fund their own growth - through the cash they generate and benefit from management teams focused on creating long-term shareholder value.

Definitions and Important Information

Unless otherwise expressly disclosed to you in writing, the information provided in this material is for educational purposes only. Any viewpoints expressed by Fidelity are not intended to be used as a primary basis for your investment decisions and are based on facts and circumstances at the point in time they are made and are not particular to you. Accordingly, nothing in this material constitutes impartial investment advice or advice in a fiduciary capacity, as defined or under the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 or the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, both as amended. Fidelity and its representatives may have a conflict of interest in the products or services mentioned in this material because they have a financial interest in the products or services and may receive compensation, directly or indirectly, in connection with the management, distribution, and/or servicing of these products or services, including Fidelity funds, certain third-party funds and products, and certain investment services. Before making any investment decisions, you should take into account all of the particular facts and circumstances of your or your client's individual situation and reach out to a professional adviser, if applicable.

CHARACTERISTICS

Earnings-Per-Share Growth Trailing measures the growth in reported earnings per share over trailing one- and five-year periods.

Earnings-Per-Share Growth (IBES 1-Year Forecast) measures the growth in reported earnings per share as estimated by Wall Street analysts.

Median Market Cap identifies the median market capitalization of the portfolio or benchmark as determined by the underlying security market caps.

Price-to-Book (P/B) Ratio is the ratio of a company's current share price to reported accumulated profits and capital.

Price/Cash Flow is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months cash flow per share.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio (IBES 1-Year Forecast) is the ratio of a company's current share price to Wall Street analysts' estimates of earnings.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio Trailing is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months earnings per share.

Return on Equity (ROE) 5-Year Trailing is the ratio of a company's last five years historical profitability to its shareholders' equity. Preferred stock is included as part of each company's net worth.

Sales-Per-Share Growth measures the growth in reported sales over the specified past time period.

Weighted Average Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the average equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Weighted Median Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the median equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

IMPORTANT FUND INFORMATION

Relative positioning data presented in this commentary is based on the fund's primary benchmark (INDEX) unless a secondary benchmark is provided to assess performance.

INDICES

It is not possible to invest directly in an index. All indices represented are unmanaged. All indices include reinvestment of dividends and interest income unless otherwise noted.

Russell 3000 Growth Index is a market capitalization-weighted index designed to measure the performance of the broad growth segment of the US equity market. It includes those Russell 3000 Index companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth rates. Effective March 24, 2025, the index applies a capping methodology. Index constituents are capped quarterly so that no more than 22.5% of the indexes weight may be allocated to a single constituent, and the sum of the weights of all constituents representing more than 4.5% of the index should not exceed 45% of the total index weight. For periods prior to March 24, 2025, the index was uncapped.

Russell 3000 Value Index is a market capitalization-weighted index designed to measure the performance of the broad value segment of the U.S. equity market. It includes those Russell 3000 Index companies with a lower price-to-book ratio and lower forecasted growth rate.

MARKET-SEGMENT WEIGHTS

Market-segment weights illustrate examples of sectors or industries in which the fund may invest, and may not be representative of the fund's current or future investments. They should not be construed or used as a recommendation for any sector or industry.

RANKING INFORMATION

(C) 2025 Morningstar, Inc. All rights reserved. The Morningstar information contained herein: (1) is proprietary to Morningstar and/or its content providers; (2) may not be copied or redistributed; and (3) is not warranted to be accurate, complete or timely. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Fidelity does not review the Morningstar data and, for mutual fund performance, you should check the fund's current prospectus for the most up-to-date information concerning applicable loads, fees and expenses. % Rank in Morningstar Category is the fund's total-return percentile rank relative to all funds that have the same Morningstar Category. The highest (or most favorable) percentile rank is 1 and the lowest (or least favorable) percentile rank is 100. The topperforming fund in a category will always receive a rank of 1% .% Rank in Morningstar Category is based on total returns which include reinvested dividends and capital gains, if any, and exclude sales charges. Multiple share classes of a fund have a common portfolio but impose different expense structures.

RELATIVE WEIGHTS

Relative weights represents the% of fund assets in a particular market segment, asset class or credit quality relative to the benchmark. A positive number represents an overweight, and a negative number is an underweight. The fund's benchmark is listed immediately under the fund name in the Performance Summary.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN STATISTICS

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (leSs) than 1 is more (leSs) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. RSquared ranges from 0 , meaning no correlation, to 1 , meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

Before investing in any mutual fund, please carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For this and other information, call or write Fidelity for a free prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus. Read it carefully before you invest.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.
